meta.description: First on-site evaluation to classify water damage, identify immediate hazards, determine water category, detect visible or suspected mold, establish scope, and trigger appropriate engineering controls before any extraction, demolition, or drying activities begin.
meta.purpose: Reduce cognitive load and prevent cross-contamination or exposure by forcing systematic hazard recognition and control selection at the very start of the job.
meta.intended_use: Loaded into field AI voice assistant. Walk technician through steps verbally. Support decision branching without requiring the tech to memorize standards.

safety_first.upgrade_triggers: Category 3 water, visible mold, strong odors, confined space, or any item from safety-red-flags.md

field_techniques.title: Signed Authorization Before Any Physical Work
field_techniques.text: The material interested party (homeowner or authorized representative) must sign the work authorization and any required releases before the technician touches anything — including shutting off water or setting up equipment. No work of any kind begins until this is complete.

field_techniques.title: Source Isolation Verification
field_techniques.text: After shutting off the angle stop or main valve, verify the water is truly stopped. For a toilet supply line failure, open the tank and hold the flapper open to confirm no additional water is entering the bowl. Visual confirmation at the source is required before moving to extraction.

field_techniques.title: Early Stabilization of Sagging Ceilings
field_techniques.text: If a ceiling is visibly sagging from water weight, set up a zip pole (or equivalent support) underneath it as soon as possible — ideally before or immediately after shutting off the water. This prevents sudden collapse during extraction or while moving through the space.

field_techniques.title: Floor Protection Before Equipment Movement
field_techniques.text: Before bringing any extraction equipment, dehumidifiers, or tools through finished hallways or unaffected areas, lay down proper floor protection (Ram Board, plastic, etc.) and corner guards. This prevents tracking contamination and damaging finished surfaces during the first day of mitigation.

field_techniques.title: Disinfectant Application Before Full Survey (Category 2/3)
field_techniques.text: On Category 2 or 3 losses, apply disinfectant spray and perform surface wiping of affected areas before conducting the full moisture survey or moving extensively through the space. This reduces cross-contamination risk and technician exposure before detailed mapping begins.

field_techniques.title: Realistic Day 1 Scope — Mitigation + Drying Setup
field_techniques.text: On an emergency water loss, Day 1 is typically limited to: signed paperwork, source isolation, bulk water extraction, basic containment where it meaningfully reduces air volume, and drying equipment setup (air movers + dehumidifiers). Full demo, detailed moisture mapping, and aggressive wall/ceiling removal usually happen on Day 2 after the equipment has run for 12–24 hours.

field_techniques.title: Initial Moisture Readings Are on Surface Materials
field_techniques.text: On first arrival, moisture meter readings are taken on whatever material is currently exposed (carpet, vinyl, tile, etc.). You cannot reliably read the subfloor until the surface material is removed or lifted. Document readings accordingly and note the material being measured.

flow.title: Arrival, Staging & Initial Safety Check
flow.voice_prompt: Arrived on site. First, do a 360 of the exterior for safety hazards like downed power lines, structural damage, or blocked access. Any immediate dangers? Remember — no physical work begins until authorization is signed.
actions: Park vehicle safely with egress clear
Perform 360 exterior walk for obvious structural, electrical, or gas hazards
Identify staging area upwind and uphill from affected zone if possible
Don minimum PPE
Confirm work authorization and releases are signed before touching anything (including shutting off water or setting up equipment)

decision_point.question: Any immediate life-safety or structural hazards preventing safe entry?

yes_path.action: STOP. Secure perimeter. Contact appropriate emergency services or utility company. Do not enter structure until cleared.

flow.title: Customer Interview & Loss History
flow.voice_prompt: Talk to the customer or responsible party. When did the water event start? What was the source? Any previous water issues or known mold? How many rooms affected? Any health symptoms reported?
actions: Record date/time of loss and discovery
Identify claimed water source (roof leak, appliance, flood, sewer, etc.)
Note duration of exposure and any prior attempts to mitigate
Ask about occupant health complaints (respiratory, skin, etc.)
Document any known pre-existing conditions in the structure

flow.title: Exterior & Perimeter Assessment
flow.voice_prompt: Walk the full perimeter. Check roofline, gutters, grading, foundation, and any obvious water intrusion points. Note standing water, erosion, or drainage issues.
actions: Inspect roof, flashing, gutters, and downspouts for obvious defects
Check soil grading and drainage away from foundation
Look for exterior cracks, efflorescence, or water stains on foundation
Note any standing water or poor drainage that could affect drying

flow.title: Interior Non-Intrusive Walkthrough
flow.voice_prompt: Enter carefully. Do a visual and olfactory scan without touching or disturbing materials yet. Look up, down, and all around. Smell for musty odors. Watch for sagging ceilings that may need immediate stabilization.
actions: Visual scan of ceilings, walls, floors, and contents for water stains, discoloration, buckling, or warping
Check for visibly sagging ceilings — if present, plan to stabilize with zip pole as soon as water is shut off
Smell test for musty, sewage, or chemical odors
Note temperature and humidity feel (hot/humid vs cool/dry)
Identify all potentially affected rooms and approximate square footage
Look for signs of previous water damage or repairs
red_flag_triggers: Buckled or saturated drywall that may collapse
Electrical outlets or panels in affected area showing water intrusion

flow.title: Hazard Identification & Water Category Determination
flow.voice_prompt: Based on what you've seen and smelled, classify the water. Is it clean (Category 1), gray (Category 2), or black/contaminated (Category 3)? Any visible mold? On Category 2 or 3, plan to apply disinfectant and surface wipe before full survey.
actions: Determine water category using core/categories.md criteria
Document justification for category choice
Check for visible mold or conditions conducive to mold (wet + organic material + time)
On Category 2 or 3 losses, apply disinfectant spray and perform surface wiping before conducting full moisture survey
Run safety-red-flags.md checklist mentally or via assistant

water_category.1: Clean water from sanitary source. Lower containment likely sufficient.
water_category.2: Gray water with some contamination. Elevated PPE and possible containment.
water_category.3: Black water or grossly contaminated. HIGH RISK. Must initiate containment-and-controls.json before any extraction or disturbance.

visible_mold_or_strong_musty_odor.true: Treat as potential mold job. Immediately trigger procedures/containment-and-controls.json and consider S-520 level protocols. Upgrade to full-face respirator or PAPR if available.

flow.title: Source Isolation & Early Stabilization
flow.voice_prompt: Shut off the water at the source. Verify it has actually stopped. If a ceiling is sagging, stabilize it with a zip pole as soon as the water is off. Lay floor protection in high-traffic areas before moving equipment.
actions: Shut off water at angle stop or main valve
Verify water has truly stopped (e.g. open toilet tank and hold flapper for supply line failures)
If ceiling is visibly sagging, set up zip pole or equivalent support immediately
Lay down floor protection (Ram Board, plastic, corner guards) in high-traffic unaffected areas before bringing in equipment
Document source isolation and any stabilization actions taken

flow.title: Engineering Controls Decision Point
flow.voice_prompt: Stop here. Based on category, mold presence, or any red flags, do we need full containment, negative air, or source isolation before we touch anything?
actions: If Category 3, visible mold, confined space, or safety-red-flags triggered → PAUSE all intrusive work
Reference procedures/containment-and-controls.json for setup requirements (barriers, negative pressure, HEPA air scrubbing, electrical isolation, etc.)
Establish containment perimeter before moving equipment or removing materials

flow.title: Moisture Detection & Preliminary Mapping
flow.voice_prompt: Now that controls are decided or in place, begin non-destructive moisture detection. Use moisture meter and thermal imager if available. Map affected areas. Remember that initial readings are on surface materials only.
actions: Use pinless or pin-type moisture meter on walls, floors, baseboards
Take readings in affected and adjacent unaffected areas for comparison
Document that initial readings are on exposed surface materials (carpet, vinyl, tile, etc.) — subfloor readings come later
Use thermal camera to identify hidden moisture patterns if available
Document all readings with locations (sketch or photo with annotations)
Identify hidden moisture in cavities, under flooring, or in insulation
flow.note: Do not cut or remove materials until engineering controls are active if Category 3 or mold present.

flow.title: Scope Definition & Job Documentation
flow.voice_prompt: Summarize findings. What is the affected area? What category? What controls are needed? What is the recommended next step? Set realistic expectations — Day 1 is usually mitigation and drying setup, not full demo.
actions: Photograph all key findings (before any disturbance)
Create simple moisture map or annotated floor plan
State water category and justification clearly
Define engineering control plan selected
Estimate affected square footage and affected materials (drywall, carpet, hardwood, insulation, etc.)
Note any contents that need immediate attention or protection
Communicate realistic Day 1 scope to customer (signed paperwork, source control, bulk extraction, basic containment, drying setup)

required: Initial moisture readings and map (noting surface materials measured)
Realistic Day 1 plan communicated to customer
